back_translation: Dinah, the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob, went out to visit the daughters of the land.
When Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, saw her, he took her, lay with her, and violated her.
His soul was drawn to Dinah, daughter of Jacob. He loved the young girl and spoke tenderly to her.
Shechem said to his father Hamor, "Get me this girl as a wife."
Jacob heard that Shechem had defiled his daughter Dinah, but his sons were with his livestock in the field, so Jacob held his peace until they came.
Hamor, Shechem's father, went out to Jacob to speak with him.

When Jacob's sons heard, they came in from the field. The men were grieved and very angry, because Shechem had committed an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob's daughter — a thing that should not be done.
Hamor spoke with them, saying, "My son Shechem's soul longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as a wife.
Intermarry with us; give us your daughters and take our daughters for yourselves.
Settle with us, and the land shall be before you. Live, trade in it, and acquire holdings in it."
Shechem said to her father and her brothers, "Let me find favor in your eyes, and whatever you say to me, I will give.
Demand of me a great bride-price and gift, and I will give whatever you say to me; only give me the girl as a wife."
Jacob's sons answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully, because he had defiled Dinah their sister.
They said to them, "We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to a man who is uncircumcised, for that would be a disgrace to us.
Only on this condition will we agree with you: if you become like us, that every male of you be circumcised.
Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves, and we will dwell with you and become one people.
But if you will not listen to us and be circumcised, we will take our sister and go."
Their proposal seemed good in the eyes of Hamor and of Shechem son of Hamor.
The young man did not delay to do this thing, because he was delighted with Jacob's daughter, and he was the most respected of all his father's house.
Hamor and Shechem his son went to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city, saying:
"These men are at peace with us. Let them dwell in the land and trade in it; behold, the land is broad enough for them. Let us take their daughters as wives and give them our daughters.
Only on this condition will the men consent to dwell with us, to be one people: if every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised.
Will not their livestock, their possessions, and all their animals become ours? Only let us consent to them, and they will dwell with us."
All who went out of the gate of his city listened to Hamor and to Shechem his son, and every male was circumcised — all who went out of the gate of his city.
On the third day, while they were in pain, two of Jacob's sons — Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers — took their swords, came upon the unsuspecting city, and killed every male.
They killed Hamor and Shechem his son with the edge of the sword, took Dinah out of Shechem's house, and went out.
The sons of Jacob came upon the slain and plundered the city because they had defiled their sister.
Their flocks, their herds, and their donkeys, and what was in the city and what was in the field — they took.
All their wealth, all their little ones, and their wives they captured and plundered, along with all that was in the houses.

Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, "You have brought trouble upon me by making me a stench among the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites and Perizzites. I am few in number; if they unite against me and attack me, I and my house will be destroyed."
But they said, "Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?"
